danger_signals.meaning: loss of offensive advantage; defensive vulnerability

danger_signals.visual_form: isometric view showing player proximity to end zone
danger_signals.meaning: distance from scoring opportunity or defensive threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: isometric field view with all players visible; ball position central to action
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: continuous player movement; play execution in real-time

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: play card system for 4-player mode; hidden selection interface to prevent opponent viewing
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: players point to play card without revealing selection on-screen

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: lined-up players on field; audio cue 'Ready! Set! Hut, hut..'
field position advancement; audio cue 'First down!'
player in end zone; audio cue 'Touchdown!'
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: score increment; return to play selection

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nester (Nintendo Power mascot) appearance as commentator

environmental_storytelling.example: angled field view creates spatial depth perception; allows display of all 22 players simultaneously; differentiates player positions and distances
distinct sprite coloring for each of eight teams; enables quick visual identification of possession and alliance on field
primitive digitized voice callouts ('Touchdown!', 'First down!', 'Ready! Set! Hut, hut..') reinforce play state and world progression
Nester character appearance at game end provides meta-narrative closure and Nintendo branding integration
surname, numbers, and position-specific visual indicators communicate roster composition and player roles

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: active movement toward opponent end zone; formation-based coordination
reactive positioning; attempt to intercept or tackle ball carrier
directional movement; avoidance of defensive players
collision detection management; play-state transition triggers

notes: NES Play Action Football uses city-based faction identification (Los Angeles, Denver, etc.) rather than traditional team names due to licensing constraints with the NFL Players Association. The 1989 NFL season player rosters formed the basis for roster composition. Virtual Console re-release replaced player names with fictional alternatives but retained graphics and stats. The isometric perspective was technically innovative for NES, enabling display of large numbers of moving entities. Disabled debug feature (Game Genie code AENNONNY) indicates collision detection was core to world-state manifestation. No color-coded faction identity documented in sources; differentiation occurs through sprite design alone.
sources_used: tcrf - NES Play Action Football (The Cutting Room Floor)
